title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: Survival of the Sweetest: The Evolution of Taste
content.text: For our ancestors, the sense of taste was not about culinary pleasure; it was a sophisticated survival tool. Every time a hunter-gatherer placed something in their mouth, their taste buds were performing a rapid chemical analysis. Is this high in energy? Is it poisonous? || (41) || [41] This binary system helped navigate a world where food was scarce and dangers were many [/41]. Sweetness was a signal for sugar—a vital source of fast-burning energy. Bitterness, on the other hand, was an alarm bell, as most toxic plants in nature have a bitter profile. || (42) || [42] Today, however, this evolutionary adaptation has become a liability [/42]. We live in an environment of 'caloric abundance,' where sugar is no longer a rare find in a forest, but a cheap and ubiquitous ingredient in almost every processed food. || (43) || [43] Our brains, however, haven't caught up with this change [/43]. They are still wired to crave the high-calorie rewards that were once essential for survival. This mismatch between our ancient biology and our modern diet is a primary driver of the global obesity epidemic. || (44) || [44] But sweetness and bitterness are only part of the story [/44]. Recent research has focused on 'Umami,' the savory fifth taste. Found in meat, cheese, and fermented foods, Umami was likely a signal for protein, another essential nutrient for brain development. || (45) || [45] Interestingly, our taste preferences are not entirely fixed at birth [/45]. While we have an innate preference for sweet and an aversion to bitter, we can learn to appreciate complex flavors through 'social modeling' and repeated exposure. || (46) || [46] This flexibility is what allowed our species to colonize such diverse environments [/46]. By learning to eat what was locally available—from the blubber of the Arctic to the spices of the tropics—we became the most adaptable eaters on the planet.

paragraphs.E: [42] The result is that our internal 'sensor' is now working against us [/42]. Instead of guiding us toward health, it drives us toward overconsumption of the very things that make us ill.
paragraphs.B: [41] Our taste buds are actually clusters of cells that send electrical signals directly to the brain [/41]. This allows for an almost instantaneous decision on whether to swallow or spit out a potential food source.
paragraphs.G: [44] Some scientists argue there may even be a sixth taste for fat [/44]. This would make sense from an evolutionary perspective, as fat is the most energy-dense macro-nutrient available.
paragraphs.C: [43] Food companies are well aware of this biological vulnerability [/43]. They design products with a 'bliss point'—the perfect combination of salt, sugar, and fat—that triggers a dopamine release in the brain.
paragraphs.A: [45] This is why children often reject vegetables but grow to love them as adults [/45]. Through consistent tasting and seeing others enjoy them, the brain eventually reclassifies 'bitter' as 'complex and safe.'
paragraphs.D: [46] This cultural transmission of food knowledge is unique to humans [/46]. It means we don't have to rely purely on instinct; we can build on the culinary wisdom of our ancestors.
paragraphs.F: Taste also plays a role in digestion. The moment we taste something sweet, our body begins to release insulin in anticipation of the sugar that is about to enter the bloodstream.

questions.explanation: B explica el proceso biológico (señales eléctricas al cerebro) que permite la decisión rápida mencionada en el texto principal.
E describe cómo nuestro 'sensor' interno ahora trabaja en nuestra contra, conectando con el cambio del entorno ancestral al moderno.
C conecta la vulnerabilidad biológica mencionada con el uso del 'bliss point' por parte de las empresas alimentarias.
G añade la posibilidad de un sexto gusto (grasa) a la discusión sobre el Umami y la densidad energética.
A explica cómo los niños aprenden a comer vegetales, ilustrando que las preferencias no son fijas.
D destaca la 'transmisión cultural' de conocimiento culinario como la clave de nuestra adaptabilidad planetaria.
